gloss: Loving-kindness — steadfast, loyal covenant love
body: Chesed is loyalty in action: the devotion a covenant partner keeps even when the other side falters, the way Ruth stayed with Naomi. Psalm 136 repeats it twenty-six times as the reason to give thanks — Yahweh’s loving-kindness simply does not run out.

gloss: Peace — wholeness, safety, and well-being
body: Shalom is bigger than a truce; it names a life with nothing broken and nothing missing. The priestly blessing in Numbers asks Yahweh to lift his face toward his people and give them exactly that.

gloss: Spirit, wind, or breath — an unseen moving force
body: Ruach can mean a gust of wind, the breath in a person’s lungs, or the Spirit of God, depending on context. Genesis opens with God’s ruach hovering over the waters, already active before anything else is made.

gloss: Soul — a living, breathing self
body: Nephesh names the whole living creature, not a detachable ghost inside it — when God breathes into the dust-formed man, the man himself becomes a nephesh. It covers appetite, emotion, and life itself.

gloss: Glory — weighty splendor and honor
body: Kavod carries the sense of weight, something substantial enough to be noticed and honored. When Yahweh’s glory settled visibly on Sinai, Israel watched a cloud make that weight impossible to miss for six days.

gloss: Truth — firmness, reliability, faithfulness
body: Emet is truth you can lean your weight on, from a root meaning stability. Yahweh describes himself to Moses as abundant in loving-kindness and emet, pairing steady love with unshakable reliability.

gloss: Law — instruction or teaching to live by
body: Torah is instruction more than mere rulebook, the guidance a parent gives a child. The psalmist calls those who walk according to Yahweh’s torah blameless, framing law as a path rather than a cage.

gloss: Righteousness — right standing and moral rightness
body: Tsedaqah describes conduct or standing that lines up with what is right. Abram simply believed Yahweh, and it was credited to him as tsedaqah — the word doing double duty for character and legal verdict.

gloss: Hear — to listen with attentive obedience
body: Shema means more than catching sound; it implies acting on what is heard. Israel’s central confession opens with the command itself — hear, Israel — turning listening into the first step of covenant loyalty.

gloss: Selah — a musical pause, likely for reflection
body: Selah’s exact function is debated, but it seems to mark a pause, perhaps for a musical interlude or a moment to let the previous line settle. Translators have left it untranslated for centuries, and the WEB simply keeps the word.

gloss: Praise — to boast, rave, celebrate loudly
body: Halal is the root behind hallelujah, praise combined with Yah, a short form of Yahweh’s name. Psalm 150 piles up imperatives from it, calling every instrument and every breath to that same loud celebration.

gloss: Salvation — deliverance, rescue, or victory
body: Yeshuah shares its root with the names Joshua and Jesus — rescue made personal. Moses and Miriam sang that Yahweh himself had become their yeshuah after the crossing of the sea, the deliverer identified with the deliverance.

gloss: Covenant — a binding agreement or compact
body: Berit names a solemn agreement, historically sealed with a ceremony as weighty as its terms. After the flood, Yahweh set his bow in the clouds as the sign of a berit with the earth itself, never to flood it again.

gloss: Compassion, tender mercy — a womb-deep care
body: Racham is related to the word for womb, picturing a compassion as instinctive as a parent’s for a child. The psalmist praises the God who crowns his people with loving-kindness and these tender mercies together.

gloss: Holy — set apart, sacred, morally pure
body: Qadosh marks something set apart for God, distinct from ordinary use. Isaiah hears seraphim cry it three times over — holy, holy, holy — a repetition Hebrew uses to signal the highest possible degree.

gloss: Everlasting — a long, often unbounded duration
body: Olam stretches toward a vanishing point at either end of time, translated everlasting or forever depending on direction. The psalmist uses it twice in one verse to frame God’s existence: from olam to olam, you are God.

gloss: Word — a spoken word, matter, or thing
body: Davar covers both the word spoken and the matter it concerns, so a word and an event share one label. The psalmist calls Yahweh’s davar a lamp for his feet, guidance meant to light the very next step.

gloss: Heart — the seat of thought, will, and feeling
body: Lev in Hebrew thought is less about emotion alone and more the command center of a person — mind, will, and feeling together. David asks God to create in him a clean lev, seeking renewal at that very center.

gloss: Service — work, labor, or ritual observance
body: Avodah covers everyday labor as well as sacred service, the same word for plowing a field and keeping a feast. Moses tells Israel to keep this avodah, the Passover observance, once they reach the promised land.

gloss: Faithfulness — firmness, steadiness, fidelity
body: Emunah is built on a root for firmness, the same root behind the word amen. Jeremiah’s lament still finds a reason for hope in it: Yahweh’s mercies are new every morning, and great is his emunah.

gloss: Redeem — to buy back or restore as next of kin
body: Ga’al is the verb of the kinsman-redeemer: the near relative obligated to buy back family land, free an enslaved kin, or avenge a wrongful death. Its participle, go’el, names that redeemer — and Job, stripped of nearly everything, still declares that he knows his go’el lives.

gloss: Sabbath — a rest, cessation from labor
body: Shabbat comes from a root meaning to cease or rest, naming the seventh-day pause built into creation itself. The fourth commandment tells Israel to remember this shabbat and keep it holy.

gloss: Wisdom — skill in living well and rightly
body: Chokhmah is practical skill for living, not mere information, the craft of choosing well. Proverbs insists that reverence for Yahweh is where this chokhmah begins, before any technique or cleverness.

gloss: Justice — a verdict, judgment, or fair ruling
body: Mishpat names both the act of judging and the fair outcome that judging should produce. Amos calls for it to roll on like rivers, justice as a flowing, sustained current rather than a single ruling.

gloss: Praise — a song or hymn of adoration
body: Tehillah names the praise itself, the hymn rather than the act of singing it, and gives the Hebrew title to the Psalms. Even in lament, the psalmist calls God the one who is enthroned on Israel’s tehillah.

gloss: Love — self-giving affection and benevolence
body: Agape is love expressed through commitment and action rather than mere feeling, the word John reaches for to say God himself is love. Paul’s famous description in Corinthians spells out exactly what that love does and refuses to do.

gloss: Word — a spoken word, message, or reason
body: Logos can mean a spoken word, an account, or the reasoning behind it, a rich term Greek philosophy had already been using for centuries. John reaches for it to open his Gospel, identifying that Word with God from the very beginning.

gloss: Grace — undeserved favor and kindness
body: Charis names a favor freely given, not earned or owed. Paul tells the Ephesians they have been saved through faith by exactly this charis, a gift rather than a wage.

gloss: Fellowship — shared participation and partnership
body: Koinonia describes a partnership held in common, more than casual friendship. Luke describes the earliest believers continuing steadfastly in the apostles’ teaching and this koinonia, alongside shared meals and prayer.

gloss: Repentance — a change of mind and direction
body: Metanoia is more than regret — it is a changed mind that redirects a life. Paul tells the Ephesian elders he testified everywhere to metanoia toward God and faith toward the Lord Jesus.

gloss: Faith — trust, conviction, or persuasion
body: Pistis is confident trust, the conviction that acts on what it cannot yet see. Hebrews opens its great chapter on the subject by defining pistis as assurance of things hoped for and proof of things not seen.

gloss: Hope — confident expectation
body: Elpis is expectation with confidence behind it, not a wish but a settled forward look. Paul writes that this elpis does not disappoint, because God’s love has already been poured into believers’ hearts.

gloss: Peace — harmony, wholeness, and calm
body: Eirene echoes the Hebrew shalom, naming a wholeness deeper than the mere absence of conflict. Jesus tells his disciples he leaves them this eirene, giving it in a way the world cannot.

gloss: Life — life, especially in its fullest sense
body: Zoe often points past mere biological existence toward life in its fullest, most vital sense. Jesus contrasts the thief who comes only to steal and destroy with himself, who came so that people might have zoe abundantly.

gloss: Glory — visible splendor, honor, or renown
body: Doxa names a splendor so apparent it demands recognition, honor made visible. Paul uses it to describe exactly what every person falls short of: all have sinned and fall short of the doxa of God.

gloss: Assembly — a gathered congregation, the church
body: Ekklesia names people called together into one assembly, a term used for both civic gatherings and, in the New Testament, the church. Jesus tells Peter he will build his ekklesia, and the WEB renders it plainly as assembly.

gloss: Good news — the gospel message
body: Euangelion is simply good news, a term used in the ancient world for any welcome announcement before it became the label for the Christian message. Mark opens his account by naming it the euangelion of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.

gloss: Counselor — one called alongside to help
body: Parakletos names one called alongside — whether as advocate, comforter, or counselor. Jesus promises his disciples this parakletos, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in his name.

gloss: Save — to rescue, heal, or deliver
body: Sozo covers rescue from danger, healing from sickness, and deliverance from sin, all under one verb. Jesus tells Zacchaeus’s onlookers that the Son of Man came to seek and to sozo what was lost.

gloss: Salvation — rescue, safety, deliverance
body: Soteria is the noun form of rescue, the state of having been brought to safety. Paul is not ashamed of the good news, he tells the Romans, because it is the power of God for soteria to everyone who believes.

gloss: Heart — the inner self, mind, and feelings
body: Kardia names the inner person behind outward behavior, thought and feeling together. Paul tells the Romans that believing in the kardia and confessing with the mouth go hand in hand.

gloss: Blessed — supremely happy or fortunate
body: Makarios describes a flourishing, fortunate state that outward circumstances cannot fully explain. Jesus opens the Beatitudes by pronouncing it over the poor in spirit, the meek, and the merciful in turn.

gloss: Servant — one who attends to or ministers
body: Diakonos names someone who waits on others in practical service, the root behind the English word deacon. Paul commends Phoebe to the Romans as a diakonos of the assembly at Cenchreae.

gloss: Apostle — one sent out with a message
body: Apostolos names a delegate sent with authority to represent the one who sent him. Paul introduces himself to the Romans as a servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostolos and set apart for the good news.

gloss: Witness — one who testifies to what is seen
body: Martys names someone who testifies to what they have seen or experienced — the word that gave English ‘martyr’ once testimony began to cost lives. Jesus tells his disciples they will be his witnesses, his martyres, to the ends of the earth.

gloss: To empty — to make void, to set aside
body: Kenoo means to empty or make void, to set something aside rather than lose it by force. Paul describes Christ who, existing in the form of God, emptied himself and took the form of a servant.

gloss: Sin — a failure or missing of the mark
body: Hamartia names a failure to hit what one aims at — by New Testament times, the everyday word for sin, wrongdoing that falls short of God’s intent. Paul states the stakes starkly: the wages of hamartia is death, but God’s free gift is eternal life in Christ Jesus.

gloss: Righteousness — right standing, moral rightness
body: Dikaiosyne names both right conduct and right standing before God, the same breadth as its Hebrew counterpart tsedaqah. Jesus calls those who hunger and thirst for dikaiosyne blessed, promising they will be filled.

gloss: Endurance — steadfast perseverance under pressure
body: Hypomone is the staying power to remain under pressure rather than escape it, patience with backbone. Paul traces a chain from suffering to this hypomone, and from there to proven character and hope.

gloss: Compassion — a gut-deep, moved-to-act pity
body: Splanchnizomai points to the inward parts, describing compassion felt so strongly it moves a person to act. Seeing the crowds harassed and scattered like sheep without a shepherd, Jesus felt exactly that toward them.
